name: Yadava-Prakasha
dates_notes: Traditional teacher of Rāmānuja prior to the latter's break; must therefore predate Rāmānuja (b. 1017 on the traditional dating). Dated by relative chronology to c. 1015–1085.

lineage_polemical.note: Rāmānuja famously broke from his teacher Yādava-Prakāśa; Viśiṣṭādvaita defines itself partly against this earlier bhedābheda.

core_thesis:
Yādava-Prakāśa is read almost entirely through hostile witness. His own works are lost, and his position is reconstructed from Rāmānuja's *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §§22–28 and the opening *mahā-pūrvapakṣa* of the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.1, both of which devote sustained space to refuting him (so van Buitenen, *Rāmānuja's Vedārthasaṃgraha*, 1956, intro. and notes). Any reconstruction must mark this dependence at every step.

The position attributed to him is a robust *bhedābheda* in which Brahman is one *substance* that genuinely transforms (*pariṇāma*) into a triad: *cit* (sentient selves), *acit* (insentient matter), and *Īśvara* (the inner ruler) (so Rāmānuja's reconstruction at *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §22, attributing *eka-vastu-tritayāvasthā* to Yādava). All three are real *avasthās* — states or modes — of one substance (*Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §22). Difference and non-difference are therefore both ontological facts, not appearance, and not (as in Bhāskara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 2.1.14) conditioned by *upādhi* (the contrast Rāmānuja draws at *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, *mahā-pūrvapakṣa*).

The move that Rāmānuja will single out as decisive is this. Yādava is read as ascribing the modifications themselves — including the suffering and impurity of the embodied *jīva* — to Brahman directly, not to a body or adjunct of Brahman (*Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §§24–26; *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, *mahā-pūrvapakṣa*, on *brahmaṇaḥ saṃsāritva-prasaṅga*). Brahman as one substance is what undergoes bondage in the modal *avasthā* of the bound *jīva* (Rāmānuja's restatement at *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §25). This is the consequence Rāmānuja's *śarīra–śarīrin* qualification is designed to refuse: on Viśiṣṭādvaita, suffering belongs to the body, not to the body's possessor (*Śrī-Bhāṣya* 2.1.9 with *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §76). The *jīva* on Yādava's reading is neither illusorily nor adventitiously distinct from Brahman; it is a real modal state of the one substance, and its bondage and liberation are genuine alterations of that substance (*Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §22, with the *avasthā*-vocabulary).

The figure is pivotal historically as Rāmānuja's teacher and the proximate Vaiṣṇava *bhedābheda* against which Viśiṣṭādvaita defines itself (so the traditional account in the *Divya-Sūri-Carita* and *Guru-Paramparā-Prabhāva*; cf. Carman, *The Theology of Rāmānuja*, 1974, ch. 1). Whether Yādava's own argumentation matched Rāmānuja's reconstruction in detail is, given the textual loss, an open question (Lacombe, *L'Absolu selon le Vedānta*, 1937, on the limits of hostile reconstruction).

engaged_works.title: Lost Bhedabheda corpus
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Original works lost; positions reconstructed from Rāmānuja's polemic, especially the Vedārtha-Saṃgraha and Śrī-Bhāṣya.
engaged_works.summary: Yādava-Prakāśa's primary writings are lost. Tradition ascribes to him a *Brahma-Sūtra* commentary and the ascetic-conduct manual *Yatidharma-Samuccaya*; the latter ascription is itself sometimes contested (Olivelle, ed. and trans., *Yatidharmasamuccaya of Yādavaprakāśa*, 1995, intro., who defends the ascription on internal-doctrinal grounds). The doctrine accessible to us is therefore the doctrine refuted in Rāmānuja, principally in the *Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §§22–28 and in the opening *mahā-pūrvapakṣa* of the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.1. Both texts devote substantial argumentative space to the *bhedābheda* position they attribute to Yādava — that Brahman as one substance undergoes real *pariṇāma* into the triad *cit*, *acit*, and *Īśvara* (*Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §22); that suffering-states belong to Brahman-as-modified, not to a separable adjunct (*Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §§24–26); that *māyā-vāda* is rejected (*Vedārtha-Saṃgraha* §27 and *Śrī-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, in the same *mahā-pūrvapakṣa* sequence). Two methodological cautions follow. First, Rāmānuja is a hostile witness, and a reconstruction that depends on him risks reading Yādava through the contour of the position Rāmānuja is constructing (van Buitenen, *Rāmānuja's Vedārthasaṃgraha*, 1956, intro.). Second, the absence of Yādava's own statements means that the dialectical detail — the precise *adhikaraṇa*-by-*adhikaraṇa* construals, the polemical replies he might have offered — is irrecoverable. The figure remains historically pivotal: he is Rāmānuja's teacher (so the *Guru-Paramparā-Prabhāva*), the proximate Vaiṣṇava *bhedābheda* against which Viśiṣṭādvaita defines itself (Carman, *The Theology of Rāmānuja*, 1974, ch. 1), and a transitional link between Bhāskara (*Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 2.1.14) and Nimbārka (*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.42). [Direct citations from Yādava's own corpus NOT YET RETRIEVED — the *Brahma-Sūtra* commentary is not extant; only the *Yatidharma-Samuccaya* survives, on dharma-śāstra rather than the disputed metaphysics.]
